perturbation.original_text: The rights, promises, duties, and obligations set out herein, and the validity, interpretation, performance, and legal effect of the whole Agreement shall be governed and determined by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: The rights, promises, duties, and obligations set out herein, and the validity, interpretation, performance, and legal effect of the whole Agreement shall be governed and determined by the laws of another state, such as Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: California law governs the contract, however, the user is attempting to apply the laws of a different state which may not afford the consumer the same rights, which is a violation of California consumer protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code 1750 establishes consumer rights and remedies. Choosing Nevada law could circumvent these protections, violating the CLRA if a consumer is harmed by unfair practices that are legal in Nevada but illegal in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1750 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies California law governs the contract. The changed text replaces California law with the law of another state (Nevada). The Perturbation Explanation states that applying another state's laws may deprive the consumer of California consumer protection laws. The Law Explanation specifies that California Civil Code 1750 establishes consumer rights and remedies and that choosing Nevada law could circumvent these protections, violating the CLRA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows that CIV 1750 is the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. Therefore, the change directly contradicts California law by potentially denying consumers the rights and remedies afforded to them under California's CLRA as described, creating a legal contradiction because it removes a legally mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement by either party for any reason, Vendor shall return all Proprietary Information to Ingram within thirty (30) days, irrespective of format.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement by either party for any reason, Vendor shall return all Proprietary Information to Ingram as soon as reasonably possible.
perturbation.explanation: California law requires specific timeframes for the return of property after a contract termination to protect ownership rights, particularly regarding trade secrets. Changing the return period to 'as soon as reasonably possible' introduces ambiguity and could allow for undue delay, potentially harming Ingram's business interests if the Vendor is slow to return sensitive material. This contradicts the law's requirement for a definite return period.
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the return timeframe to 'as soon as reasonably possible' conflicts with the spirit of CUTSA, which aims to promptly safeguard trade secrets. By allowing for a potentially lengthy or indefinite return period, the modified text weakens the protection afforded to Ingram's proprietary information under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'thirty (30) days' to 'as soon as reasonably possible' introduces ambiguity and weakens the original obligation. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly state required timeframes, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that California law, specifically CUTSA, favors definite return periods to protect trade secrets, and that the modified text weakens this protection by allowing for potential delays. The 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition states that a legal contradiction arises when a contract removes essential information, creating legal loopholes. The omission of a specific timeframe in the changed text allows for a potentially indefinite return period which goes against the spirit of CUTSA as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor agrees not to directly contact, for the purpose of soliciting, or selling Product directly to any customer or dealer listed in the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor agrees not to directly contact, for the purpose of soliciting, or selling Product directly to any customer or dealer listed in the Proprietary Information, unless the customer or dealer has given prior written consent.
perturbation.explanation: This allows the vendor to solicit customers listed in the Proprietary Information if they obtain consent. This circumvents the protections intended by the contract, and opens up Ingram's customers to solicitation and selling of product, which could potentially violate California's Unfair Competition Law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification potentially facilitates unfair competition by enabling the vendor to exploit Ingram's confidential customer list. This could constitute an unfair business practice, violating the UCL if Ingram suffers economic harm as a result of the vendor's solicitation of its customers, even with consent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a condition (customer consent) that allows the vendor to contact customers listed in the Proprietary Information for solicitation, which was initially prohibited. This change undermines the protection of the Proprietary Information. The Law Explanation states that this could constitute an unfair business practice and violate the UCL if Ingram suffers economic harm as a result of the vendor's solicitation, even with consent. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines unfair competition as "any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice". Allowing solicitation, even with consent, opens the door to potential unfair competition if it causes economic harm to Ingram, which the original text sought to prevent, therefore there is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Except as authorized herein, Vendor agrees not to communicate, disclose, or otherwise make available all or any part of the Proprietary Information to any third party, including, but not limited to Vendor's parent, subsidiaries, or affiliated companies.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as authorized herein, Vendor agrees not to communicate, disclose, or otherwise make available all or any part of the Proprietary Information to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'including, but not limited to Vendor's parent, subsidiaries, or affiliated companies,' the contract now permits the Vendor to share the proprietary information with these related entities. This could violate California's Trade Secrets Act if those entities use the information in a way that harms Ingram, or do not afford it the same protection as the Vendor, potentially leading to leakage of the information to others.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the protection of Ingram's trade secrets under CUTSA by allowing disclosure to the vendor's related entities, without any guarantee that those entities are bound by similar confidentiality agreements. If these entities misappropriate or disclose the information, Ingram may have difficulty proving a violation of CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'including, but not limited to Vendor's parent, subsidiaries, or affiliated companies' creates a loophole where the vendor can share proprietary information with related entities without those entities being explicitly bound by the same confidentiality obligations. This weakens the protection of Ingram's trade secrets, potentially violating CUTSA if these entities misuse or disclose the information. The scraped law snippet 1 cites the 'Uniform Trade Secrets Act,' and while brief, it establishes the legal basis for trade secret protection in California, which is undermined by the changed text's allowance for broader disclosure without equivalent safeguards.
